verses.book: The Epistle of Theonas, Bishop of Alexandria, to Lucianus, the Chief Chamberlain.
verses.text: Therefore you ought to strive to the utmost of your power not to fall into a base or dishonourable, not to say an absolutely flagitious way of thinking, lest the name of Christ be thus blasphemed even by you.

verses.book: The Epistle of Theonas, Bishop of Alexandria, to Lucianus, the Chief Chamberlain.
verses.text: Be it far from you that you should sell the privilege of access to the emperor to any one for money, or that you should by any means place a dishonest account of any affair before your prince, won over either by prayers or by bribes.

verses.book: The Epistle of Theonas, Bishop of Alexandria, to Lucianus, the Chief Chamberlain.
verses.text: Let all the lust of avarice be put from you, which serves the cause of idolatry rather than the religion of Christ.

verses.book: The Epistle of Theonas, Bishop of Alexandria, to Lucianus, the Chief Chamberlain.
verses.text: No filthy lucre, no duplicity, can befit the Christian who embraces the simple and unadorned Christ.

verses.book: The Epistle of Theonas, Bishop of Alexandria, to Lucianus, the Chief Chamberlain.
verses.text: Let no scurrilous or base talk have place among you.

verses.book: The Epistle of Theonas, Bishop of Alexandria, to Lucianus, the Chief Chamberlain.
verses.text: Let all things be done with modesty, courteousness, affability, and uprightness, so that the name of our God and Lord Jesus Christ may be glorified in all.

verses.book: The Epistle of Theonas, Bishop of Alexandria, to Lucianus, the Chief Chamberlain.
verses.text: Discharge the official duties to which you are severally appointed with the utmost fear of God and affection to your prince, and perfect carefulness.

verses.book: The Epistle of Theonas, Bishop of Alexandria, to Lucianus, the Chief Chamberlain.
verses.text: Consider that every command of the emperor which does not offend God has proceeded from God Himself; and execute it in love as well as in fear, and with all cheerfulness.

verses.book: The Epistle of Theonas, Bishop of Alexandria, to Lucianus, the Chief Chamberlain.
verses.text: For there is nothing which so well refreshes a man who is wearied out with weighty cares as the seasonable cheerfulness and benign patience of an intimate servant; nor, again, on the other hand, does anything so much annoy and vex him as the moroseness and impatience and grumbling of his servant.

verses.book: The Epistle of Theonas, Bishop of Alexandria, to Lucianus, the Chief Chamberlain.
verses.text: Be such things far from you Christians, whose walk is in zeal for the faith.

verses.book: The Epistle of Theonas, Bishop of Alexandria, to Lucianus, the Chief Chamberlain.
verses.text: But in order that God may be honoured in yourselves, suppress ye and tread down all your vices of mind and body.

verses.book: The Epistle of Theonas, Bishop of Alexandria, to Lucianus, the Chief Chamberlain.
verses.text: Be clothed with patience and courtesy; be replenished with the virtues and the hope of Christ.

verses.book: The Epistle of Theonas, Bishop of Alexandria, to Lucianus, the Chief Chamberlain.
verses.text: Bear all things for the sake of your Creator Himself; endure all things; overcome and get above all things, that ye may win Christ the Lord.

verses.book: The Epistle of Theonas, Bishop of Alexandria, to Lucianus, the Chief Chamberlain.
verses.text: Great are these duties, and full of painstaking.

verses.book: The Epistle of Theonas, Bishop of Alexandria, to Lucianus, the Chief Chamberlain.
verses.text: But he that striveth for the mastery is temperate in all things; and they do it to obtain a corruptible crown, but we an incorruptible.
